headlines.title: WOMAN COULD NOT SIT UP WITH FAMILIES
headlines.subtitle: Now Does Her Own Work. Lydia E. Pinkham's Vegetable Compound Helped Her.
headlines.fullText:
Ironton, Ohio .- "" I am enjoying better health now than I have for twelve years. When I began to take Lydia E. Pinkham's Vegetable Compound I could not sit up. had female troubles and was very nervous. I used the remedies a year and I can do my work and for the last eight months I have worked for other women, too. I cannot praise Lydia E. Pinkcham's Vegetable Compound enough for I know I never would have been as well if I had not taken it and I recommend it to suffering women."

Why will women continue to suffer day in and day out and drag out a sickly, missing three fourths of the joy of living, when they can find health in Lydia E. Pinkham's Vegetable Compound ?
If you have the slightest doubt write to Lydia E. Pinkham Medicine Co. Lynn, Mass. for advice. Your letter will be opened, read and answered by a woman and held in strict confidence.

headlines.title: MANY VETERANS VISIT REUNION
headlines.subtitle: Full Week Awaits Confederates Who Go to Park-Many Features Arranged.
headlines.fullText:
A large party of Confederate veterans and their families went out Saturday to Confederate Park, near the Lake Worth dam, where the annual reunion of the veterans has just started. No formal program had been arranged for Saturday, the day being devoted to pitching tents and getting camp in shape for the coming week.
Sermon Sunday by Rev. Z. T. Morris, chaplain of the Texas department. Judge C. C. Cummings will act as master of ceremonies.
Monday will be Daughters Day, with addresses by Mrs. L. Jordan, president of Julia Jackson chapter, United Daughters of the Confederacy, Mrs. J. D. Covert, historian of the chapter; Mrs. Felix Walther, who has been prominent in its work.
Tuesday has been designated as Old Maids' Day. The speakers will be Gen. K. M. Van Zandt, Albert J. Raskin and John I. Terrell. Saturday will be the "getaway" day.
Wednesday's program will include addresses by Judge W. P. McLean Sr. and Judge Marvin H. Brown.
The speakers for Thursday will include Judge C. C. Cummings, R. F. Peeples, L. H. Bursa and Judge Jesse M. Brown.
Friday has been designated as Old Maids' Day. The speakers will be Gen. K. M. Van Zandt, Albert J. Raskin and John I. Terrell. Saturday will be the "getaway" day.
Arrangements have been made for an hourly automobile service from the end of the Rosen Heights car line to the camp ground, beginning at 9:30 a. m. each day during the reunion. Several amusement features have been installed, together with booths which will furnish refreshments.

headlines.title: 400 Gather for Elks' Big Barbecue at Lake Erie
headlines.subtitle: Nothing Doing for the Fishes When Hungry Crowd Gets Busy-Dancing Follows Feast.
headlines.fullText:
The spooniest moon of the summer shone on 400 Elks, Elkesses and their dears dears at what was the most heavily attended exclusive Elks affair in the history of the Fort Worth lodge.
The "deep-sea" fish in Erie's waters had been dieting for weeks in anticipation of the affair, but they were sorely disappointed. Hungry Elks and Elkesses in hordes that surprised the kitchen force and there were no scraps left over for the finny tribe. Even the waiters, all Elks, dropped their tools when the starting bell rang and Castle-walked to the kitchen to feed themselves while maitre d'hotel had difficulty convincing these committeemen he was really serious when he gave such commands to them as "Wash this tub of knives" and "You cubist artists get busy with this cheese."
Music Breaks It Up.
Just as the feeding was delayed, the orchestra came to the rescue with the tingling tune of "You're Here and I'm Here," the crowd swarmed over the waxed floor.
For nearly two hours, the dancing went on uninterrupted. Then Maitre d'hotel Ben Keith announced "Watermelon," and it seemed like ages before the barbecue had been served.
As to dancing, the old-timers who stepped off on the floor Friday night did so with all the confidence of a Vernon Castle. They demonstrated, furthermore, that one may hesitate, one-step, tango, Castle-walk or waltz to any of the latter-day dance tunes and the "Croonie Melody" was fairly limped cored until the orchestra quit from sheer lack of wind.
Honor guests of the affair were Walter Keith's efficient staff of waiters, who appeared on the scene dolled up in dress suit and buggy and expecting to look important and have a good time, and appointed on "committees" to wait on the tables. The Traction Company provided special car service and the speed was up to date.
In conjunction with General Chairman Keith, R. D. Hunter, secretary of the Elks lodge, arranged and directed the barbecue and dance.

headlines.title: COTTON MILLS EXPECTING BIG GAIN IN TRADE
headlines.subtitle: Demand for American Goods Will Boom South's Textile Industry.
headlines.fullText:
Special to The Star-Telegram.
CHARLOTTE, N. C. Sept. 5 .- All of the Cannon mills, which are located at Concord, N. C, and elsewhere, were running regularly last week. No general closing down order has gone out. Some of these mills will run three days per week; some for a longer time. General closing is not contemplated. No unusual stocks of goods on hand are reported.
The Highland Park Mills of Charlotte resumed operation.
The Orr Cotton Mills of Anderson, S. C., which shut down for two weeks, have gone back to work.
J. E. Effird of the Effird mills, Albemarle, N. C., reports that his mill has orders for nearly 2,000,000 pounds of cotton yarn.
The Locke mills of Concord has resumed operations. All of the mills in Gastonia, N. C. are running regularly.
Numerous Southern mills are curtailing now, but in the majority of cases this curtailment is merely in line with the vacation policy general at this season of the year.
S. B. Tanner, former president of The American Cotton Manufacturers Association, reports that there is no overproduction at the mills and that any curtailment is temporary.
James A. Greer, editor of the Textile Manufacturer of Charlotte, is optimistic when asked for an opinion: "The South is soon to see the greatest period of prosperity that has ever come to a textile center. There will be an unprecedented demand upon mills of this country to supply goods for the world.
"From all reports that have come to me, and we are in close touch with the situation, there are very few, if any, mills curtailing at present on account of the war situation.
"I received in this morning's mail a letter from J. D. Massey, treasurer of the Eagle and Phoenix Mills of Columbus, Ga., setting forth a very optimistic view of the outlook for his mills, which are among the largest in the South."

headlines.title: CREDIT ASSOCIATION ELECTS NEW DIRECTORS
headlines.subtitle: G. E. Lewis and B. W. Campbell, New Members of Board-Officers to Be Picked Later.
headlines.fullText:
Nine of the eleven directors of the association were elected Friday night and the newly organized directors will meet soon to elect a president, a vice president and a secretary and treasurer. The new directors are: G. E. Lewis and B. W. Campbell. The directors re-elected are: William Monnig, Mattye Best Palmer, R. H. Martin, W. C. Stripling, J. Montgomery Brown, R. E. Covey, Leon Gross, John Deem and W. G. Burton.
Secretary Kerr reported the association's indebtedness has been reduced from $1,200 to $300 within the last year and has handled more than 25,000 inquiries. F. E. Crittenden, in a brief talk, said the European war has bred a spirit of confidence among business men here and that co-operation has been advanced by the crisis rather than destroyed.
The Grocers and Butchers Association met after the credit men's meeting and resolved in favor of closing groceries and butcher shops all day Labor Day.

headlines.title: Made $10 Grow Where One Grew Before; Sought
headlines.fullText:
Miss Ernestine Hounshel, clerk at the Cigar Nook, Sixth and Main streets, sold a well dressed stranger a 10-cent cigar Friday and then gave him back $9.90 in exchange for a $10 bill. The stranger-a heavy set, dark man in his shirt sleeves-thanked her, smiled and walked out after touching a match to his cigar.
Later Miss Hounshel happened to think that the bill did not look like a $10 bill at all but a $1 bill. Detectives were called in, but they have been unable to trace the stranger. He had raised the $1 bill to a crude looking ten spot by taking the corner from some other note and pasting it behind the "1" on the forged note.

headlines.title: RAILROAD NOTES
headlines.fullText:
Fort Worth railroad offices will grant their employes a holiday Monday. The shop and freight offices and the general offices will close Monday at 1 p. m.
War Bulletins on Train.
The Santa Fe, which set a precedent for other roads when it put a matron on its California Limited to chaperon young girl travelers, is now posting war bulletins on this train, according to advices received at the city ticket office. The bulletins are posted at half a dozen points between Chicago and California.
To Revise Rates.
The meeting of Texas rate clerks, set for Sept. 8 in Galveston, has been postponed until after the meeting of the general passenger agents in St. Louis. The rate meeting is to revise the tariffs between North and South Texas, in accordance with the new mileage book which was lengthened after the T. & B. V. ceased business out of Fort Worth and Dallas.
Pacific Coast Rate $32.50.
Tickets to Pacific coast points will be on sale from Sept. 24 to Oct. 8 at the rate of $32.50.
The agents do not expect a heavy travel to the coast this fall, however. They say Texas people are waiting to go to the Panama Canal Exposition in San Francisco next year.

headlines.title: ORPHANS' HOME WORK IS PROGRESSING WELL
headlines.subtitle: Building Should Be Finished Within Sixty Days, Commissioner Thinks - Ready by First of Year.
headlines.fullText:
Very good progress is being made on the rebuilding of the Fort Worth Orphans Home, and County Commissioner I. E. Doringer said he was satisfied with the way in which the work is progressing.
With fair weather the building should be finished within sixty days and that it should be ready for occupancy by the first of the year at the outside.

headlines.title: ASKS REWARD FOR TEXAS BOY'S BRAVERY
headlines.subtitle: Governor's Wife Writes Carnegie Commission Regarding Max Reitzch.
headlines.fullText:
AUSTIN, Sept. 5 .- Mrs. O. B. Colquitt yesterday wrote to the Carnegie Hero Fund Commission asking it to investigate the bravery of Max Reitsch, who rescued a girl from drowning in the surf.
The boy is but 10 years old.

headlines.title: 2 BOYS KILLED WHEN TRAIN HITS WAGON
headlines.fullText: RUSSELLVILLE, Ark., Sept. 5. - Two boys were killed here yesterday when a train hit their wagon. The driver either clung to the lines but the wagon was demolished and one mule, which survived the accident, dragged him to safety.

headlines.title: GRAND JURY PROBING SELLING TO MINORS
headlines.subtitle: Investigation From Charges of Two Boys Results in Juvenile Custody.
headlines.fullText:
An investigation of alleged sales of liquor to minors is being made by the grand jury in connection with stories told by Paul Jenkins, 15, and Fred Little, 14, who are now in the custody of officers of the juvenile court.
An information charging selling to minors was filed in the county court Friday against Billie Leroy, said to be a motion picture operator in a Main street show. The boys went to the place Friday to identify the man who they say made the sale.

headlines.title: ARRESTED IN MEXICO
headlines.subtitle: El Paso Doctor Imprisoned Following Death of Patient.
headlines.fullText:
EL PASO, Sept. 5. Dr. N. T. Moore, an American, is in jail at Juarez, Mexico, and his wife here has asked Secretary Bryan to try to obtain his release.
Moore was arrested after he had performed an operation, the patient dying.
